content.text: The four golden signals — latency, traffic, errors, and saturation — emerged from Google's Site Reliability Engineering practice as the minimal, sufficient set of measurements needed to understand whether a service is healthy. Applied to agentic AI systems they require re-interpretation: an agent is not a stateless HTTP handler. It orchestrates multi-step reasoning, calls external tools, accumulates context across turns, and consumes token budgets at variable rates. Each signal maps to several distinct sub-signals in an agentic context, and getting the mapping right is the difference between meaningful SLOs and vanity metrics.
What It Is
The
four golden signals

are a monitoring framework for any user-facing service. They answer four questions simultaneously: How slow is it? How much work is it doing? How often does it fail? Is it running out of some resource? Together, they provide a complete first-order picture of service health and are the first metrics to instrument before building specialised dashboards or alerting stacks. For agentic AI, they extend naturally across the orchestration layer, the LLM inference layer, and the tool execution layer.
Why It Matters

Without a shared signal vocabulary, teams instrument whatever is convenient — typically internal counters like GPU memory usage or queue sizes — which measure causes, not symptoms. When an agent degrades, the symptoms a user experiences (slow responses, task failures, partial tool results) may lag behind the internal cause by minutes. Structuring monitoring around the four golden signals ensures you always have a user-visible SLI to alert on, regardless of which internal component is the root cause.
Benefits
Provides a complete, minimal monitoring baseline. You can operate an agentic system with dashboards covering only these four signals if necessary.
Enables SLO definition from day one. Each signal maps directly to an SLI that can be budgeted.
Facilitates consistent on-call runbooks: every alert can be classified under a signal, making triage structured rather than ad-hoc.
Supports capacity planning: traffic and saturation signals together give direct headroom measurements.
Cross-team alignment: a shared vocabulary reduces time spent arguing about what to measure.
Trade-offs

The four signals cover user-visible health but not quality. An agent that responds fast, with low errors, but consistently produces wrong answers is invisible to these signals alone — quality signals (eval scores, hallucination rates) must be added alongside them.

For agentic AI, latency has high natural variance because of multi-step reasoning and stochastic LLM generation. Setting meaningful SLOs requires percentile-based budgets (p95, p99) rather than averages.
Saturation is harder to measure for agentic systems than for traditional services because the "resource" can be a soft limit (token rate cap) rather than a physical one (CPU threads).
Architecture

attrs.alt: Four quadrant diagram showing latency, traffic, errors, and saturation signals for agentic AI systems with sub-signals, SLO targets, and PromQL examples for each

content.text: Each of the four quadrants maps a golden signal to its agentic AI sub-signals, SLO targets, and the PromQL expression used to measure it. Together the four quadrants cover the complete health surface of an agent-based service.
Signal 1: Latency

Latency measures how long a user waits for a response. For a stateless HTTP service, a single end-to-end request duration suffices. For an agentic system, latency decomposes into several distinct sub-signals that must each be tracked separately to identify bottlenecks.
Latency Sub-signals
End-to-end agent response time
: The duration from user message received to final agent response delivered. This is the primary user-visible latency SLI. Measured as a histogram with buckets at 1s, 2s, 5s, 8s, 15s, 30s.
LLM time-to-first-token (TTFT)
: The duration from sending a completion request to receiving the first output token. This governs the perceived responsiveness of streaming interfaces. Target: p99 TTFT < 2s.
Tool call duration per hop
: Duration of each external tool invocation (database query, API call, code execution). High tool call latency often dominates total response time in tool-heavy agents.
Multi-hop latency budget

: The total latency across all reasoning hops (each LLM call + tool call sequence). An agent that makes five tool calls with a 1s budget per hop consumes 5s before returning. Multi-hop count must itself be tracked and capped to maintain the end-to-end budget.

# Prometheus metric definitions for latency signals

# End-to-end agent response time histogram
- metric_name: agent_session_duration_seconds
  type: histogram
  labels: [agent_name, model, tenant_id]
  buckets: [0.5, 1, 2, 5, 8, 15, 30, 60]
  description: >
    End-to-end duration of one agent session from first user turn
    to final response, in seconds.

# LLM time-to-first-token histogram
- metric_name: llm_ttft_seconds
  type: histogram
  labels: [model, provider]
  buckets: [0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 5]
  description: >
    Duration from LLM request sent to first token received (streaming).

# Tool call duration per tool type
- metric_name: agent_tool_call_duration_seconds
  type: histogram
  labels: [tool_name, agent_name]
  buckets: [0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10]
  description: Duration of each tool invocation.

# Reasoning hops per session
- metric_name: agent_session_hops_total
  type: histogram
  labels: [agent_name]
  buckets: [1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21]
  description: Number of LLM-call / tool-call cycles in one session.
Signal 2: Traffic
Traffic measures how much demand the system is receiving. For agentic AI, traffic has multiple dimensions that all matter independently for capacity planning.
Traffic Sub-signals
Agent sessions per second
: New sessions initiated per unit time. The primary request rate for the orchestration layer.
Concurrent active sessions
: Sessions that are currently in-flight (waiting for LLM or tool responses). This governs concurrency limits and connection pool sizing.
Token throughput
: Input and output tokens per second across all sessions. This is the primary capacity metric for the LLM provider tier, not session count, because a single session can consume thousands of tokens.
Tool invocations per minute
: The rate of calls to downstream tools. Critical for capacity planning of each tool dependency (database, vector store, external APIs).

# Sessions per second (primary traffic signal)
rate(agent_sessions_total[5m])

# Concurrent active sessions (gauge)
agent_sessions_active

# Input token throughput (tokens/second)
rate(llm_input_tokens_total[5m])

# Output token throughput (tokens/second)
rate(llm_output_tokens_total[5m])

# Tool invocations per minute
rate(agent_tool_calls_total[1m]) * 60

# Alert: traffic 3x above baseline (unexpected spike)
# Uses a 1-week lookback for baseline
alert: AgentTrafficSpike
expr: |
  rate(agent_sessions_total[5m])
  > 3 * avg_over_time(rate(agent_sessions_total[5m])[7d:5m])
for: 5m
labels:
  severity: warning
annotations:
  summary: "Agent session rate is 3x the 7-day baseline"
Signal 3: Errors

Errors measure the fraction of requests that fail. For agentic AI, "error" requires a more precise definition than for HTTP services: the agent may return a 200 OK HTTP response while still having failed to complete the user's task (e.g., because it hit a tool timeout and returned a partial result). Errors must therefore be classified at both the infrastructure level and the application level.
Error Sub-signals
Agent session failure rate
: The fraction of sessions that end in a terminal error state (exception, timeout, guardrail block that cannot be resolved). This is the primary error SLI.
Tool call error rate per tool
: Error rate broken down by tool name. A spike in database tool errors is distinct from a spike in web search tool errors.
LLM refusal rate
: The fraction of LLM calls where the model refuses the request (content filter, prompt injection detection). High refusal rates signal input distribution shift or a policy change at the provider.
Timeout rate per hop
: Separately tracks LLM call timeouts, tool call timeouts, and session-level timeouts. Timeout patterns distinguish between provider degradation and tool dependency failures.
Guardrail block rate
: Output safety check failures. Elevated block rates after a model upgrade indicate the new model generates content outside the policy envelope more frequently.

# Session failure rate (primary error SLI)
rate(agent_sessions_total{status="error"}[5m])
/ rate(agent_sessions_total[5m])

# Tool error rate by tool (for per-tool alerting)
rate(agent_tool_calls_total{status="error"}[5m])
/ rate(agent_tool_calls_total[5m])

# LLM refusal rate
rate(llm_calls_total{finish_reason="content_filter"}[5m])
/ rate(llm_calls_total[5m])

# Timeout rate (all components)
rate(agent_timeouts_total[5m]) / rate(agent_sessions_total[5m])

# SLO: error rate < 1% over 30-day rolling window
# Error budget burn rate alert (3x burn rate for 1-hour window)
alert: AgentErrorBudgetBurn
expr: |
  (
    rate(agent_sessions_total{status="error"}[1h])
    / rate(agent_sessions_total[1h])
  ) > (3 * 0.01)
for: 5m
labels:
  severity: page
annotations:
  summary: "Agent error rate burning error budget at 3x rate"
Signal 4: Saturation

Saturation measures how "full" the service is — the utilisation of the constraint resource. When saturation reaches 100%, the service becomes unavailable or severely degraded. For agentic AI, the constraint resources are often soft limits (token rate caps set by LLM providers) or structural limits (context window size) rather than physical hardware limits.
Saturation Sub-signals
Token rate limit headroom
: (rate_limit - current_rate) / rate_limit. When this drops below 20%, the system is approaching throttling. Expressed as a utilisation fraction [0,1].
Context window utilisation
: tokens_in_context / model_context_limit. Sessions that approach the context window limit must truncate history or fail, directly affecting output quality and reliability.
Agent queue depth
: Number of sessions waiting to be scheduled. Queue growth above a threshold indicates the orchestration layer cannot keep up with incoming traffic.
Worker concurrency utilisation
: active_sessions / max_concurrent_sessions. When this approaches 1.0, new sessions are queued rather than served immediately.
Embedding queue lag
: For retrieval-augmented agents, the latency between embedding request and result. Lag growth indicates the embedding service is saturated.

# Token rate limit utilisation (0-1 fraction, where 1 = fully saturated)
rate(llm_input_tokens_total[1m]) / llm_token_rate_limit_per_minute

# Context window utilisation per active session
llm_context_tokens_used / llm_context_limit

# Agent queue depth (absolute)
agent_queue_depth

# Worker concurrency utilisation
agent_sessions_active / agent_max_concurrent_sessions

# Alert: token rate utilisation > 80%
alert: TokenRateLimitSaturation
expr: |
  rate(llm_input_tokens_total[1m])
  / llm_token_rate_limit_per_minute > 0.8
for: 2m
labels:
  severity: warning
annotations:
  summary: "LLM token rate is above 80% of limit; throttling risk"

# Alert: context window > 85% utilised in active sessions
alert: ContextWindowSaturation
expr: |
  avg(llm_context_tokens_used / llm_context_limit) > 0.85
for: 5m
labels:
  severity: warning
annotations:
  summary: "Average context window utilisation exceeds 85%"
How to Implement
OpenTelemetry Instrumentation
Use the OpenTelemetry Python SDK to emit all four signals as spans and metrics. The OTel SDK exports to Prometheus via the OTLP exporter or the Prometheus exporter.

"""Four golden signal instrumentation for an agentic AI session."""
from opentelemetry import metrics, trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics import MeterProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.export import PeriodicExportingMetricReader
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.metric_exporter import OTLPMetricExporter
import time

# --- Metric definitions (all four signals) ---
meter = metrics.get_meter("agent.golden_signals", version="1.0")

# LATENCY — histograms
session_duration = meter.create_histogram(
    "agent_session_duration_seconds",
    unit="s",
    description="End-to-end agent session duration",
)
llm_ttft = meter.create_histogram(
    "llm_ttft_seconds",
    unit="s",
    description="LLM time-to-first-token",
)
tool_call_duration = meter.create_histogram(
    "agent_tool_call_duration_seconds",
    unit="s",
    description="Duration of each tool call",
)

# TRAFFIC — counters and gauge
sessions_total = meter.create_counter(
    "agent_sessions_total",
    description="Total agent sessions started",
)
sessions_active = meter.create_up_down_counter(
    "agent_sessions_active",
    description="Currently active agent sessions",
)
llm_input_tokens = meter.create_counter(
    "llm_input_tokens_total",
    description="Total LLM input tokens consumed",
)
llm_output_tokens = meter.create_counter(
    "llm_output_tokens_total",
    description="Total LLM output tokens generated",
)

# ERRORS — counters with status label
tool_calls_total = meter.create_counter(
    "agent_tool_calls_total",
    description="Tool call attempts, labelled with status",
)
timeouts_total = meter.create_counter(
    "agent_timeouts_total",
    description="Timed-out operations (llm, tool, session)",
)

# SATURATION — gauges
context_tokens_used = meter.create_gauge(
    "llm_context_tokens_used",
    description="Tokens currently used in the context window",
)
queue_depth = meter.create_gauge(
    "agent_queue_depth",
    description="Sessions waiting to be scheduled",
)


def run_agent_session(agent_name: str, model: str, tenant_id: str) -> None:
    attrs = {"agent_name": agent_name, "model": model, "tenant_id": tenant_id}
    sessions_total.add(1, attrs)
    sessions_active.add(1, attrs)
    start = time.monotonic()
    try:
        # ... orchestration logic ...
        sessions_total.add(1, {**attrs, "status": "success"})
    except Exception:
        sessions_total.add(1, {**attrs, "status": "error"})
        raise
    finally:
        sessions_active.add(-1, attrs)
        session_duration.record(time.monotonic() - start, attrs)
Setting SLOs on Each Signal

SLOs translate golden signals into contractual commitments. Each SLO has an SLI (the measurement), an objective (the threshold), and an error budget (the allowed deficit over a time window). The following table gives representative starting SLOs for an agentic AI service.
Signal
SLI
Objective
Notes
Latency
p95 end-to-end session duration
< 8s (99% of 30-day window)
Adjust per use case; chat agents tolerate higher latency than automation
Latency
p99 LLM TTFT
Controls perceived streaming responsiveness
Traffic
Provisioned capacity / peak traffic
> 2x headroom
Capacity planning target, not an alert
Errors
Agent session failure rate
< 1% over 30-day window
Primary reliability SLO
Errors
LLM refusal rate
Alert on sudden spikes (provider policy change)
Saturation
Token rate limit utilisation
Alert at 80%; provision more capacity or route to alternative model
Saturation
Avg context window utilisation
Sessions above 85% risk truncation errors
Common Mistakes
Measuring only average latency. Averages mask the long tail: p95 or p99 latency is the right SLI for agentic systems where outliers are frequent and user-visible.

Using HTTP response codes as the sole error signal. An agent that returns 200 OK with a failure message in the body is invisible to HTTP-based error tracking. Define an application-level error counter based on session outcome, not HTTP status.

Measuring traffic only in sessions per second. Token throughput matters independently: one session with a 10,000-token context window consumes 100x the LLM capacity of one session with a 100-token context. SLO capacity planning must account for token rate, not just session count.

Ignoring context window saturation. When a session fills its context window, the agent either truncates history (quality impact) or fails (reliability impact). Both outcomes are invisible without a context utilisation metric.
Setting SLOs only on end-to-end latency without per-component budgets. If the budget is 8s total but no tool call budget is set, a single slow tool call can consume the entire budget undetected.
Best Practices
Instrument at every layer independently — session, LLM call, tool call — so each layer has its own latency and error histograms.
Set per-component latency budgets: if the end-to-end SLO is 8s, allocate a sub-budget to each LLM call and each tool call and alert when any sub-budget is breached.
Cap the maximum number of reasoning hops per session as both an operational guard and a direct latency control.
Use error budget burn rate alerts (multi-window: 1h fast burn, 6h slow burn) rather than instantaneous threshold alerts on error rate.
Track LLM refusal rate and guardrail block rate as distinct error signals; a spike in either after a deployment indicates a model or policy regression, not a pure reliability incident.
Monitor token rate limit headroom across all LLM provider accounts, not just the primary account. If you use multiple accounts for fan-out, saturation can hide in the aggregate.
Performance & Scalability

Histogram metrics for latency have non-trivial cardinality cost if you add many label dimensions. Keep latency histograms at most three labels (agent_name, model, tenant_id). Avoid per-session-id labels on histograms — they create unbounded cardinality. For per-session analysis, use trace spans rather than metrics. Keep traffic counters to two or three label dimensions and use aggregate dashboards; per-user rate analysis belongs in log analytics, not Prometheus.
Tooling & Ecosystem
Prometheus — primary storage for all four signals as counters, histograms, and gauges. Use recording rules to pre-compute frequently used rate expressions.
Grafana — SLO dashboards, per-signal time series, and golden signal overview panel sets.
OpenTelemetry SDK — unified instrumentation for traces and metrics, with OTLP export to Prometheus-compatible backends.
Sloth (sloth.dev) — SLO definition and error budget burn rate alert generation from simple YAML SLO specs.
Pyrra — Kubernetes-native SLO operator that generates Prometheus recording rules and alerts from SLO CRDs.
Mental Model

Think of the four signals as four gauges on an aircraft instrument panel: airspeed (traffic), altitude loss rate (latency), engine fault lights (errors), and fuel remaining (saturation). Any one of them failing to reach safe range is worth landing for. In agentic AI, the analogy holds: you should be able to look at the four golden signal dashboards and know within 60 seconds whether the system is healthy, degraded, or in incident — without drilling into individual component metrics. Those component metrics are the
diagnostic instruments
you reach for after the golden signals tell you something is wrong.
Key Takeaways
The four golden signals — latency, traffic, errors, saturation — provide a complete, minimal monitoring baseline for any agentic AI service.
Latency in agentic AI decomposes into end-to-end session time, LLM TTFT, tool call duration, and multi-hop count; each must be tracked separately.
Traffic must be measured in token throughput as well as session rate, because token consumption per session is highly variable.
Errors must be measured at the application level (session outcome) not just HTTP level, because a 200 OK can still represent a task failure.
Saturation includes soft limits (token rate caps) and structural limits (context window size), not just physical hardware limits.
Set SLOs on each signal and use multi-window error budget burn rate alerts to catch both fast and slow degradations.
The four signals answer "is the service healthy?" — quality signals (eval scores) answer "is the service correct?" and must be measured alongside them.
